Description (from Project): Funding Stellar smart wallets today requires G-address intermediaries because C-addresses need contract deployment before receiving funds—a chicken-and-egg problem. The C-Address Toolkit abstracts this complexity: developers pass a C-address, and the SDK handles resolution, deployment, and funding in one call. We're delivering TypeScript/Python SDKs plus a reference wallet demonstrating the complete passkey onboarding flow
Products & Services:
> Important Note: This toolkit is a pragmatic bridge for C-address adoption, not a replacement for native CEX support. Key clarifications:
    >
    > - This is NOT asking CEXs to change — The proxy approach works with existing G-address withdrawal systems. No changes required from exchanges or on-ramps.
    > - This is NOT a consumer wallet product — The reference wallet is a developer demonstration, not a Freighter competitor. It shows how to build C-address experiences.
    > - The proxy does NOT store private keys — Keypairs are derived on-demand within the contract's execution context and discarded after signing. No keys are ever stored or exposed.
    > - This is developer infrastructure — Like TypeScript binding generators or SDK libraries, this enables other developers to build C-address-first applications.
    
    ---
    
    Current Stellar tooling assumes G-addresses for funding flows. This creates a gap: developers building with Smart Wallets cannot easily enable external deposits to C-addresses from exchanges, fiat on-ramps, or other wallets. CEXs and on-ramps only support G-addresses—they cannot send directly to C-addresses.
    
    Why Proxy? CEXs and fiat on-ramps won't add native C-address support in the near term—it requires changes to their withdrawal systems, compliance flows, and address validation logic. The proxy approach enables C-address funding today without requiring any changes from external services. Users get a standard G-address they can share anywhere, while funds automatically arrive at their C-address. This is the pragmatic bridge that unlocks Smart Wallet adoption while the ecosystem matures.
    
    ```
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                         G-to-C Proxy Flow                               │
    ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    │                                                                         │
    │   CEX / On-ramp         Proxy G-Address           C-Address (Smart Wallet)
    │        │                      │                          │              │
    │        │   1. Withdraw        │                          │              │
    │        │─────────────────────▶│                          │              │
    │        │   (standard G-addr)  │                          │              │
    │        │                      │                          │              │
    │        │                      │  2. Relayer detects      │              │
    │        │                      │     (Horizon stream)     │              │
    │        │                      │                          │              │
    │        │                      │  3. Forward funds        │              │
    │        │                      │─────────────────────────▶│              │
    │        │                      │  (relayer signs tx)      │              │
    │        │                      │                          │              │
    │   No changes needed     Deterministic            User receives funds    │
    │   to CEX/on-ramp        (no key storage)         in < 30 seconds        │
    │                                                                         │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    ```
    
    G-to-C Proxy Contract + Relayer: A two-component system enabling CEX/on-ramp deposits to C-addresses:
    
    a. Registry Contract (Soroban): Stores ONLY the mapping C-address → Proxy G-address. No secrets, no keys, no signing logic—just a lookup table.
    
    a. Relayer Service: Monitors Horizon for payments to registered proxy addresses. When payment detected:
    - Derives the proxy keypair on-demand using HKDF: keypair = HKDF(master_seed, c_address, "stellar-proxy-v1")
    - Signs the forwarding transaction
    - Submits to Horizon
    - Immediately wipes keypair from memory
    
    Security Model:
    - Master seed held by relayer operator (in HSM for production, or self-managed for self-hosters)
    - No keys stored in contract or relayer database—derived on-demand, wiped after use
    - Destination locked in registry—relayer cannot redirect funds
    - Self-hostable: operators can run their own relayer with their own seed
    - Deterministic: same C-address always produces same proxy G-address (verifiable)
    - Open source: full audit trail, anyone can verify the code
    
    See PROXYCONTRACTSPEC.md for complete technical specification including cryptographic details, threat analysis, and implementation checklist.
    
    ### Trust Model & Security Boundaries
    
    Hosted Relayer Trust Assumption:
    
    When using the hosted relayer, you trust the relayer operator to correctly forward funds during the forwarding window (~30 seconds). The relayer operator holds the master seed and has technical capability to derive proxy keypairs.
    
    The registry contract provides verifiable destination lookups but does not cryptographically enforce forwarding behavior. Enforcement relies on:
    - Operator reputation and legal accountability
    - Open-source code (verifiable behavior)
    - HSM audit logs (detectable malfeasance)
    - Self-hosting option (trustless alternative)
    
    Recommendations by Use Case:
    
    | Use Case | Recommended Setup |
    |----------|-------------------|
    | Casual users, small amounts | Hosted relayer (convenience) |
    | High-value transfers | Self-hosted relayer |
    | Enterprise/institutional | Self-hosted with HSM |
    | Maximum security | Verify each forward before next deposit |
    
    ---
    
    C-Address Funding SDK (TypeScript + Python): Core library enabling direct C-address funding. Includes proxy address generation, transaction building, address resolution, and Smart Wallet authentication integration. Published on npm and PyPI.
    
    > Ecosystem Compatibility Note: We're tracking the December 2025 shift toward OpenZeppelin's smart-account-kit as the recommended smart wallet standard. The SDK v1.0 will integrate with passkey-kit (production-ready, battle-tested). The architecture is designed to support future compatibility with smart-account-kit as that ecosystem matures—the proxy system and funding flows are wallet-agnostic and will work with any C-address regardless of the underlying smart wallet implementation.
    
    Reference Wallet (Next.js PWA): Production-grade Progressive Web App demonstrating C-address onboarding and funding flows. Core features at Freighter parity: token balances (SEP-41), transaction history, send/receive flows, passkey auth (Face ID, Touch ID), mobile installable (PWA), testnet + mainnet support.
    
    > Cross-Device Passkey Strategy: The reference wallet relies on platform-native passkey synchronization (iCloud Keychain for Apple devices, Google Password Manager for Android/Chrome). This is the standard approach recommended by FIDO Alliance and provides seamless cross-device access within each ecosystem. Limitations: cross-ecosystem access (e.g., iPhone to Windows) requires manual QR-code-based passkey sharing. The onboarding documentation will clearly communicate these platform boundaries and provide guidance for users with multi-platform needs. Future enhancement: support for adding multiple passkey signers to a single C-address (requires smart wallet policy configuration).
    
    Onboarding Standards + SEP Draft: Developer guide with integration patterns for exchanges and on-ramps, plus a proposed SEP for C-address discovery and funding protocols submitted to stellar/stellar-protocol.
    
    Integration Examples: Working code for exchange deposits (via proxy), fiat on-ramp integration guide, and AI agent funding flows.

Description (from Project): RFP Track - Develop a specialized reverse engineering tool that accepts a compiled Soroban Smart Contract (WASM file) and produces a highly accurate and human-readable WebAssembly Text (WAT) representation. The core of the work is to integrate deep knowledge of Soroban's internal structures and logic into the decompilation process to create a superior, more context-aware reconstruction of the contract's functionality.
Products & Services:
A Rust CLI Tool

The end product is a single binary distributed via crates.io and as pre-built binaries for Linux, macOS, and Windows. It is not a web application, browser extension, or GUI tool. Users invoke it directly from their terminal:

  $ soroban-decompile contract.wasm --format rust --output contract.rs

  $ soroban-decompile contract.wasm --format wat --verbosity detailed

  $ soroban-decompile contract.wasm --format json --output analysis.json

The CLI accepts a .wasm file and produces one of three output formats: idiomatic Rust source code (.rs), annotated WebAssembly Text (.wat), or a structured JSON analysis report (.json). Users control verbosity, annotation levels, and output paths via command-line flags. The tool is built with clap for argument parsing, and all output is deterministic for a given input.

  
The Soroban Reverse Engineering Tool is a specialized, open-source reverse engineering tool for Soroban Smart Contracts on the Stellar blockchain. The tool will accept compiled Soroban contracts (WASM files) and produce highly accurate, human-readable Rust source code by leveraging deep knowledge of Soroban’s internal structures, conventions, and SDK patterns.

Products and Services:

-   An automated accuracy benchmarking tool performing AST-based comparison against 50+ contracts, producing quantitative similarity metrics.
    
-   Optimized decompilation engine achieving 90% reconstruction accuracy.
    
-   Full Rust code generation for complex SDK patterns, nested types, and edge-case contracts.
    
-   A CLI tool with configurable output options, verbosity levels, and error reporting.
    
-   Comprehensive documentation: user guide, API reference, architecture overview, contribution guidelines, and a tutorial for extending the tool with new pattern recognizers.
    
-   Production-ready release.
    
-   Complete test suite with Soroban contracts covering all major contract patterns in the Stellar/Soroban ecosystem.

Description (from Project):
StellarScope is a high-performance aggregation service and API designed to normalize and display a user’s total decentralized finance (DeFi) exposure across the Stellar network. As Stellar’s DeFi sector matures—with total value locked across top protocols like Blend, Aquarius, and others continuing to scale—users and developers face significant complexity. Tracking an aggregated position encompassing deposits, borrowings, and yield across isolated lending pools and AMMs creates a deeply fragmented user experience.  

Our objective is to solve this by providing a unified, developer-friendly data feed. By normalizing complex protocol semantics, StellarScope empowers developers to build advanced analytics platforms and personal finance dashboards without the overhead of manual protocol integration. Within the 10-week development cycle, we will deliver a production-ready API deployment, comprehensive reference documentation (mirroring the quality of the Soroswap API docs), and a seamless self-service developer onboarding portal.
Products & Services:
1. DeFi Positions Aggregation API

What it is: A production-grade REST API that returns a user’s aggregated DeFi positions (deposits, borrows, returns, health factors) across supported Stellar protocols.

How Stellar is used:

-   LedgerCloseMeta / TransactionMeta ingestion (authoritative source) to extract all Soroban and classic protocol events.
    
-   Custom ingestion pipeline (Ingest SDK + Captive Core + data lake) to deterministically reconstruct protocol state.
    
-   Ledger sequencing + idempotent replay to compute positions per address without reliance on RPC or Horizon.
    

Impact on the project:

Enables wallets, dashboards, and analytics tools to display a complete and accurate DeFi portfolio for any Stellar address without building per-protocol indexers

2. Protocol-Specific Position Logic

What it is: Dedicated adapters that interpret protocol semantics (lending, borrowing, AMMs, vaults, backstop pools)

How Stellar is used:

-   Soroban contract event decoding (ScVal/XDR) directly from ledger metadata
    
-   LedgerEntryChange parsing to reconstruct LP shares, vault balances, and debt positions
    
-   simulateTransaction (non-authoritative) to compute derived view outputs such as Blend health factor without replicating on-chain math.
    
-   Classic trustline & liquidity pool ledger entry diffs (no Horizon dependency)
    

Impact on the project:

Guarantees position accuracy and consistency with protocol UIs while preserving ledger-level correctness and replayability.

3. Historical Pricing & USD Valuation Engine

What it is: A pricing layer that enriches on-chain positions with historical and current USD values

How Stellar is used:

-   SEP-40 oracle state read from ledger entry changes (via ingestion pipeline)
    
-   AMM reserve state changes from Aquarius and Soroswap to compute TWAP
    
-   Ledger timestamps (ledger close time) to align valuation with historical positions
    
-   CoinGecko fallback only when on-chain price unavailable (non-authoritative)
    

Impact on the project: Unlocks P&L tracking, portfolio history charts, and consistent valuation across the Stellar DeFi ecosystem while prioritizing on-chain data sources.

4. Developer Dashboard & Self-Service Onboarding

What it is: A web interface for exploring DeFi positions and onboarding developers to the API.

How Stellar is used:

-   Queries the API using Stellar G-address (and supported account formats)
    
-   Surfaces indexer lag and protocol health derived from chain tip vs last indexed ledger.
    
-   Displays data quality signals derived from ledger ingestion state.
    

Impact on the project: Reduces time-to-first-value and provides a reference UX for ecosystem tools.

5. Reliability & Data Integrity Layer

What it is: Infrastructure ensuring accurate, transparent, and resilient data delivery.

How Stellar is used:

-   Ledger-based cursor tracking for deterministic replay.
    
-   Append-only ingestion aligned with Stellar’s immutable ledger model
    
-   Chain tip comparison (ledger sequence) to surface freshness and partial data
    
-   Deterministic replay from data lake to correct discrepancies
    

Impact on the project:

Builds developer trust by making data quality, freshness, and degradation explicit

Description (from Project): A unified, semantic API that computes and serves a user’s total DeFi exposure across major Soroban protocols. The service ingests protocol state (Blend, Aquarius, Soroswap, FxDAO), normalizes positions into financial primitives (deposits, borrows, LP exposure, debt, yields), computes derived metrics (net exposure, health factor, risk signals), and provides real-time and historical position data via a performant REST interface. Orion enables wallets, dashboards, analytics tools, and risk engines to build consistent, accurate DeFi user experiences without reimplementing protocol logic.
Products & Services:
Orion is a self-contained, ledger-pinned, multi-protocol DeFi positions API with published correctness guarantees.

#### What Exists Today

StellarExpert is the dominant Stellar block explorer with a public API, it sees all on-chain activity but has no protocol enrichment. It cannot compute a health factor or decompose an LP position. LOBSTR integrates Blend and Aquarius for its 5M users but has no public API and no health factor display. Smoothie provides rich Blend analytics, yield decomposition, P&L, cost basis with a browser-dependent backend service supporting only Blend.

The structural gap: None of them can tell you an arbitrary address's health factor across all Stellar DeFi protocols via a single server-side call.

#### What Orion Delivers

A REST API with full OpenAPI spec. One call returns complete DeFi position state for any Stellar address across all supported protocols: USD-denominated collateral, liabilities, health factor, borrow limit, LP token decomposition, CDP vault collateral ratio, backstop position, yield estimates. Every response includes data_staleness_seconds, last_indexed_ledger, and attribution_confidence so consumers can assess data quality programmatically.

-  

Core Service Offerings

##### 1. Aggregation Engine & Protocol Adapters

Multi-protocol DeFi position normalization across Blend, Aquarius, Soroswap, and FxDAO. Ledger-pinned state reads via getLedgerEntries, direct ScVal decoding, wasmHash-based version detection, SEP-41 token resolution, and per-pool oracle integration. Protocol-specific semantics — l_factor inversion, bToken/dToken conversion, LP share decomposition, CDP linked list enumeration — handled inside each adapter without leaking into the core system.

##### 2. Positions & Activities API

GET /v1/users/:address/positions — health factor, borrow limit, LP decomposition, CDP collateral ratio, yield estimates, full protocol breakdown. Every response includes data_staleness_seconds, last_indexed_ledger, and attribution_confidence.

GET /v1/users/:address/activities — normalized event taxonomy (deposit, withdraw, borrow, repay, liquidation, claim_rewards) across event-driven protocols. FxDAO explicitly excluded and documented.

GET /v1/health — ingestion lag, adapter status, cache state, oracle staleness.

##### 3. Developer Surface

Auto-generated SDKs (TypeScript, Go, Python) versioned from the OpenAPI spec. CLI via npx oriondefi positions <address>. Claude and MCP-compatible agent skills exposing the full API surface to AI toolchains. Self-service developer portal with API key issuance, usage visibility, and OpenAPI explorer.

4. Correctness Artifacts & Observability

Published snapshot fixture suite, versioned JSON across all supported adapters, pinned to closed ledgers, independently verifiable against the public endpoint. Live smoke tests on every deploy: 3–5 controlled addresses per protocol, one per position archetype. Open-source contract registry auto-populated from events, with metrics and dashboards covering ingestion lag, per-adapter health, API latency percentiles, and cache hit ratio.

Architecture

Ingestion (Deliberately Phased)

The ingestion source is phased by practicality. Tranche-1 and Tranche-2 use a dedicated Goldsky Mirror,  a managed Stellar indexing service that provides full event history without Captive Core infrastructure. The adapter interface is data-source agnostic: the same LedgerCloseMeta structs arrive regardless of source. T-3 migrates to Captive Core (hot path, real-time ledger stream) plus Galexie CDP data lake (cold path, historical backfill and recovery). The adapters are unchanged by this migration, which provides a clean swap at the LedgerBackend layer in preparation for Mainnet. 

    +---------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
    | Phase               | Ingestion Source                         | Rationale                                                                                    |
    +---------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
    | Testnet MVP         | Goldsky Mirror + Soroban RPC             | Managed indexing operational in hours; Captive Core requires multi-week setup; adapter       |
    |                     |                                          | interface is data-source agnostic for a clean swap later.                                    |
    +---------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
    | Testnet Expansion   | Goldsky Mirror + Soroban RPC             | Multi-protocol expansion validates adapter correctness, not infrastructure; same source as   |
    |                     |                                          | T1.                                                                                          |
    +---------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
    | T3 Mainnet          | Captive Core (hot) + Galexie CDP (cold)  | Full SDF-native stack; eliminates third-party indexing dependency; adapters unchanged, only  |
    |                     |                                          | the LedgerBackend implementation swaps.                                                      |
    +---------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

For on-demand state reads reserve balances, oracle prices, user positions at a specific ledger.  Orion uses stellarcore.Client's GetLedgerEntries(ctx, ledgerSeq, keys...). This pin reads to the exact ledger sequence just ingested, eliminating race conditions where events at ledger N are matched with state at ledger N+2. getLedgerEntries is unaffected by the Protocol 22 SDK incompatibility that breaks simulateTransaction.

Storage

-   PostgreSQL:  normalized positions, activities, protocol metadata
    
-   TimescaleDB:  time-series position snapshots enabling historical queries and snapshot-at-ledger
    
-   ElastiCache: 30s TTL hot cache with event-driven invalidation per address
    

API Layer

-   Public tier: 60 req/min per IP, no auth required,  suitable for developers and hackathons
    
-   Authenticated tier: 600 req/min per key, configurable for high-volume integration partners
    
-   Response Metadata: data_staleness_seconds, last_indexed_ledger, partial_result, attribution_confidence, oracle_staleness_seconds (storage-polled protocols)
    

  

---

PLANNED protocol coverage

#### Blend Protocol (T1)

-   bToken/dToken share-to-asset conversion
    
-   Health factor
    
-   Borrow limit, borrow cap, net APY, supply APY, borrow APY breakdown
    
-   Full backstop decomposition: BLND/USDC exposure, Q4W countdown, emissions
    
-   V1/V2 versioning via wasmHash registry with support for different SCALAR constants handled per-pool
    

#### Aquarius AMM: T2

-   Pool discovery via router events and Pool Plane contract.
    
-   LP holder identification: LP token (TokenShare) mint/burn events.
    
-   LP decomposition: ~8 positions across 4 pools
    
-   Token metadata support via SEP-41 compatible contracts
    
-   Parity reference: on-chain pool state (TotalShares, ReserveA/B from instance storage)
    

#### Soroswap: T2

-   ~174 pairs from FactoryContract. Top pools: XLM/CDTKPWPL (~1.34M XLM), XLM/USDC (356K XLM), USDC/CDTKPWPL (282K USDC).
    
-   Pair enumeration via factory all_pairs(i).
    
-   Constant product LP math computation.
    
-   Adapter-level raw RPC fallback for Protocol 22 simulateTransaction incompatibility
    
-   Parity reference: on-chain pair reserves and TotalSupply from instance storage
    

#### FxDAO: T2

-   CDP vault protocol: deposit XLM collateral, mint USDx stablecoin (EURx, GBPx defined but inactive)
    
-   Enumerate & support  all active USD vaults.
    
-   Health factor & Oracle integration (more information & discovery needed)
    
-   Indexing: storage polling adapter with ~60 seconds linked list poll on Vaults.
    

  

In order to de-risk adapter design, every protocol adapter will be built through engagement with protocol teams early in the tranche to ensure alignment on enumeration strategies, indexing boundaries, oracle integration, event schema etc before we commit to an approach. 

  

Competitive Context
=======================

Existing Tools and where Orion fits
---------------------------------------

    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | Dimension                    | StellarExpert                 | Orion                                         | LOBSTR                       | Smoothie             |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | Type                         | Block explorer + Open API     | DeFi infrastructure API                       | Consumer wallet              | Blend analytics      |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | Protocols                    | All (no enrichment)           | Protocol agnostic + Blend + Aquarius +        | Stellar + Aquarius earn +    | Blend only           |
    |                              |                               | Soroswap + FxDAO                              | Blend earn                   |                      |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | Public API                   | Yes (rate-limited, no SLA)    | Yes (OpenAPI, SLA, versioned)                 | No public API                | No public API        |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | Open source                  | Yes (explorer)                | Yes                                           | Yes                          | Yes                  |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | LP decomposition             | No                            | Yes                                           | No                           | No                   |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | Health factors               | No                            | Yes                                           | No                           | No                   |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | CDP vaults                   | No                            | Yes                                           | No                           | No                   |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | Ledger-pinned reads          | No                            | Yes                                           | N/A                          | No                   |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+
    | Hybrid indexing (event +     | No                            | Yes                                           | No                           | No                   |
    | storage polling)             |                               |                                               |                              |                      |
    +------------------------------+-------------------------------+-----------------------------------------------+------------------------------+----------------------+

  

StellarExpert is raw infrastructure. It sees everything on-chain but understands nothing at the protocol level. It is complementary to Orion: StellarExpert is the raw ledger layer, Orion is the semantic enrichment layer above it. Smoothie is deeply capable within its scope. It produces yield decomposition, P&L, cost basis, and annualized ROI computed on top of Blend State. But its architecture makes it inaccessible as infrastructure: the browser must first read the current position state from a connected wallet and pass it as query parameters to Smoothie's server with computation via RPC. 

Orion reconstructs state across protocols and exposes it as infrastructure. Smoothie could plug into Orion as a data source. Orion cannot be replaced by Smoothie.
